chief_complaint: A 16-year-old male from a rural African region presented with a 2-week history of progressive shortness of breath and fatigue.
history: The patient had no significant past medical history and was not on any medications. He had a history of mild hypertension, which was not well-controlled. He was a non-smoker and had no history of substance abuse. He had been experiencing symptoms of heart failure for 2 weeks, including shortness of breath, fatigue, and swelling in his legs.
exam: Vital signs: BP 140/90 mmHg, HR 120 bpm, RR 24 bpm, O2 sat 92% on room air. Physical examination revealed a loud S3 gallop, bilateral lower limb edema, and a grade 2/6 pansystolic murmur at the apex.
labs: ECG: Normal sinus rhythm with left ventricular hypertrophy. Bloods: Elevated troponin T (0.5 ng/mL), normal creatinine kinase. Echocardiogram: Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) 30%, left ventricular hypertrophy, and a large pericardial effusion.
correct_diagnosis: Restrictive cardiomyopathy due to Fabry disease
differentials: Restrictive cardiomyopathy due to Fabry disease
correct_ranking: Restrictive cardiomyopathy due to Fabry disease
